content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. What movie blew you away purely based on its cinematography?

**Score:** 125
**Body:** I am looking for films where every single frame feels like a painting. Not just movies with cool special effects, but films where the camera placement, lighting, colors, and shadows tell a story all on their own. Movies like Blade Runner 2049 or Dune instantly come to mind where you could pause at literally any second and print out the frame to hang on your wall. What is a movie you recommend watching simply because of how gorgeous it looks?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Train Dreams
2. Blade Runner 2049 is a more recent one that blew me away at times. 
3. Samsara 
4. The Fall
5. 1917
6. The Fountain
7. The Secret Life of Walter Mitty
8. Lawrence of Arabia
9. Barry Lyndon 
10. The Revenant

## 2. Movies about loneliness

**Score:** 40
**Body:** I enjoyed "Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind", "Lost in translation" and "Her" and I'm looking for movies that might give off a similar loneliness feeling. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Lars and the Real Girl
2. Taxi Driver
3. The Station Agent
  - Love this film, never really see anyone mention it so very cool to see this.
4. Shame
5. Many Wong Kar Wai movies. Chungking Express, Fallen Angels, Days of Being Wild. While centered around love, there's this underlying theme of distance and isolation present throughout his movies.
6. Synecdoche, New York
7. I Saw the TV Glow
8. Adaptation 
9. A Serious Man

## 3. Survival movies that heavily involves cold weather or a setting that just involves snow in them.

**Score:** 28
**Body:** I really want to see more movies in cold climates. Something similar to the thing or the grey. It doesnt have to be really about survival.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Revenant 
2. *Alive* (1993) and *Society of the Snow* (2023) - both based on the 1973 crash of the Uruguayan Rugby team in the Andes mountains. *Alive* is considered the more Hollywood-ized version and stars Ethan Hawke, I still think it has a lot of merit, and both are worth watching. SotS is in Spanish and more closely follows one of the survivor's books, and swept the Spanish film awards a couple of years ago.
3. Wind River.

It's a murder Mystery by Taylor Sheridon, and snow/cold is very centered theme (about survival).
4. 30 Days of Night (2007)
5. *The thing* (1982).
6. The Mountain Between Us (2017) 
7. The Day After Tomorrow
8. The Terror. It's a show but it's good.
9. Arctic with Mads Mikkelsen
10. The Hateful Eight 

## 4. movies for a grieving person?

**Score:** 26
**Body:** my wife lost her father yesterday. i need to distract her all day as best as i can.

i'm looking for movies with absolutely no parent loss (excludes shrek 3 and at least 1 animated barbie movie) or i might even extend it to parent reconnection (such as mulan). lighthearted, some drama, no death or separation

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. My condolences to your family.
  - thank you, she's taking it harder than she expected to since their relationship was so complicated
    - I had a complicated relationship with my mom and her death was harder for it. She’s grieving the dad she had, but she’s also grieving the dad she wanted to have and any hope she had for change/improvement in their relationship. It’s a lot and I’m grateful you’re there to help her wade through it. So sorry and sending hugs. 
2. School of rock
3. Well definitely do NOT show her Big Fish!

My go to is Support Your Local Sheriff (1969)it's an oldie but it has James Garner, Jack Elam, Henry Morgan and it's very funny.
4. As a person with a little depresh, here are my top non-sad films & TV  
\-Clueless  
\-When Harry Met Sally  
\-Superbad  
\-Easy A  
\-Almost Famous  
\-Parks & Recreation  
\- Spirited Away (the little girl does try to save her parents, so judgement call on this)  
\- Sad Drama without Death: Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind, Doubt, La La Land

I lost my dad and had a complicated relationship too. I wish her the best in her grieving journey. hugs.
5. Blast from the Past
6. To Wong Foo Thanks for Everything, 40 Year Old Virgin, Grandmas Boy
7. How to Lose A Guy in 10 Days 

The Goonies 

Center Stage 

Barb and Star go to Vista Del Mar 

I am very sorry for her loss, one of my friends buys you a grief cake when your dad dies. You get to eat the whole cake with a fork if you want and it always has extra icing flowers. 
8. Bring it On

Legally Blonde



fluffy comedies are my go to

## 5. Movies where the good guy is way ahead of everybody

**Score:** 24
**Body:** Looking for movies where the good guys is way too clever BUT looks like he is struggling. A good example is the shawshank redemption where andy completely outsmarts every character including the audience

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Lucky Number Slevin
  - One of my favorites. Just watched it again a couple nights ago 
2. Sherlock Holmes’s and Sherlock Holmes game of shadows 
3. Tinker Tailor Soldier Spy. George Smiley is always way ahead, despite being way behind on everything else.

Both the recent-ish feature film and the original BBC miniseries are great. Having to choose between Alec Guinness and Gary Oldman is a problem of success.
4. The Thomas Crown Affair
5. The Equalizer movies
6. The Big Short(2015) 
7. The usual suspects. Depending on who you think is the good guy.
8. Catch me if you can 
9. Saltin sea

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. What movie scene shook you and made you deeply uncomfortable while watching it? For me, it was the scene between Kayla and Roger Ailes in Bombshell.

**Score:** 1452
**Body:** I'd also like to appreciate Margot Robbie and John Lithgow for their performances. They absolutely nailed their characters in the movie.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The basement scene in War of the Worlds.  Ray has to make a choice to kill another person to save his daughter and then walk back to her like nothing happened.  Does she know what he did? Will they ever talk about it?
2. ![gif](giphy|2q9bpGWGS9DkQ)
3. Lithgow is incredible, I always think of him as nice and funny, but he was so vile in Bombshell. The heavy breathing while he forced the woman to halfway strip, I wanted to leave the room entirely
  - I've seen him play a villain way too many times to see him as nice and funny, but he can be absolutely hilarious, too. He's a fantastic performer.
4. The scene in Annihilation when the main character is mimics by the alien version of herself.   
![gif](giphy|l49K22TZLuEJCjjDq)
  - You’re talking about the final \~2 scenes? I agree with you, made me deeply uncomfortable and couldn’t shake the feeling for a couple days. They made some excellent filming and audio choices as well that just deepen the feelings the viewer gets. I’m unlikely to ever watch that movie again because of the feeling it gave me. 
5. The reveal at the end of the horror movie Shutter (2004). I'm not an huge horror fan, but that creeped me out good lol.
6. My dad took me to see Untraceable (2009) and had to leave early because the torture scenes were too much for young me lol 
7. The scene between Laura Dern and Willem Defoe in Wild at Heart. "Someday, honey, I will. But I gotta get going!"
8. The eclair scene in Van Wilder. 

## 2. What is the most suspenseful/tension-filled movie scene you’ve ever watched?

**Score:** 905

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The cell phone ringing in the departed 
  - https://preview.redd.it/byh3613ie1fh1.jpeg?width=1280&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=b1c37795b92e45f84cd7b27cd744928384b540ac
2. Has to be the blood testing scene in The Thing. We don’t know who is actually the alien. Everyone is pissed at each other. People tied to benches. One dude with a flame thrower. It’s perfect with its build up.
  - "I know you gentlemen have been through a lot, but when you find the time, I'd rather not spend the winter TIED TO THIS FUCKING COUCH!!"
3. The robbery scene in Boogie Nights was amazingly tense
4. Once more I have to ask "what's the movie in OP's example"?
  - Sicario
5. Natalie Portman walking past Gary Oldman to knock on Jean Reno's door was pretty intense.
6. https://i.redd.it/uhqpg0kb81fh1.gif
  - I wish I can rewatch this again for the first time. 

## 3. What are your thoughts on Jason Isaacs? What's your favorite performance of his?

**Score:** 825

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. He was hilarious as Zhukov in Death of Stalin. 
  - https://preview.redd.it/s1fr044271fh1.jpeg?width=1168&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=0f5b7ff1604e05fa44a3d29716a30b912e57fa50
    - They actually had to give him FEWER medals than the real Zhukov had, because otherwise it would've looked too absurd
  - My favourite. "I mean, I'm smiling but I'm very fucking furious right now."
2. I liked it when he played that cold and menacing evil guy with an English accent.
3. Loved him in White Lotus all zooted up on Benzos 
4. The Patriot, he was such an insufferable ass. 
5. OA
6. I want to shout out his animation voiceover work, for example as Commander Zhao in the original Avatar: The Last Airbender. 

https://preview.redd.it/av9ebi6q61fh1.png?width=360&format=png&auto=webp&s=654aaf9c20775f5257614cb93f2af6e0d557f679

Also, the Grand Inquisitor on Star Wars Rebels. 
7. He's in one of my two favorite horror movies: Event Horizon

## 4. Thoughts on Alison Brie?

**Score:** 555

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. ![gif](giphy|J8FZIm9VoBU6Q)
  - https://preview.redd.it/646qb7z6a2fh1.jpeg?width=320&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=240edcd7ebcde2fbe53ccb96c5f97aea3736aede


2. Annie’s pretty young, we try not to sexualize her
  - Meanwhile Annie:

![gif](giphy|L61UVTEJrmqju)
    - ![gif](giphy|LeRXK9yAL50YM)
      - ![gif](giphy|bdycpgiLtvwoU)
        - https://i.redd.it/ub5a2brj32fh1.gif
  - ![gif](giphy|y9b4QeK13CoGk)
3. Dave Franco is one lucky mf’er.
4. Every interview I've seen her do, I walk away wishing I could just hang out with her. 

During Covid, they did this 'Community' script read and also posted a post-show video, which was an hour and a half of them reconnecting and shooting the shit. Donald Glover, Dan Harmon, Jim Rash, Joel McHale... she was easily the funniest person on that panel. 

## 5. Which actress is the modern equivalent of Sigourney Weaver?

**Score:** 351

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Probably Sigourney Weaver 
  - My answer was no one. Yours was equally correct 
2. There isn't one 
  - Correct answer. 
3. There will only ever be one Ellen Ripley
4. Charlize Theron was that, for a while. Mad Max Fury Road, Atomic Blonde, Hancock, Aeon Flux, The Italian Job. She’s played some badass women. 
  - Sigourney and Charlize can do comedy too
  - Apex this year. So maybe she's still that. 
5. No real modern equivalent same as Linda Hamilton in t2.

Closest might be hopefully Amber Midthunder or Samara Weaving.
6. I'm just struck by the low-level audacity of suggesting that Sigourney Weaver isn't *modern.*  I may need to lie down.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. I can't stop thinking about what Nolan crafted Troy to represent

**Score:** 38
**Body:** (Spoilers for the whole movie)

I just cannot stop thinking about how clever it was for Nolan to use the sacking of Troy to talk about standards of decency and fairness, and the violation of those standards. The idea that what happened at Troy was so horrible that the Greeks (led by Odysseus) *broke* something in the world. That this was such a moral transgression that is having almost ontological ramifications. The idea that these old standards of decency and politeness are actually important things to keep. Shockingly, it's an idea that I think is actually a little conservative, at least in the former definition of that term.

It's a message that is both very timeless, but also very timely. I'm 33 years old, and like a lot of you I have felt what has been a slow loss of trust in the future, and in our institutions. I remember when the 'villains' of the day was John McCain and Mitt Romney, and I'm certain that if you told the majority of Republicans circa 2012 what would be happening today they would be legitimately horrified. It feels like the world has become so much cruller, more insane, that people have become more irrational. No one has hope anymore and because of that they've decided they're going to do their small part to make the world a WORSE place.

You can give a lot of answers as to what our "sacking of Troy" is that's caused this. And to be fair life is not so simple, there is not one single cause. And hopefully even not everyone everywhere feels what I'm feeling. And I'm sure more will happen that will fit the bill in the future. For me though? It's people who voted for trump in 2025. When he and the republicans had made clear who they were and what they wanted. Do the democrats suck? Was it a big pain in the ass to have to do lockdown for X number of years because of Covid? Is your pension a thing of the past? Yes. Duh. But in your selfishness and your *intentional* stupidity you've taken the "human misery lever" and decided to push it FORWARD rather than doing what small things you can to hold it back. Why did you do this? Why Joe Rogan, why Theo Von? Why did you go "Well I'm a little pissy. I'm going to put a gun to everyone's head, including myself, and pull the trigger?"

You people broke the world. You are worse than Germans who voted Hitler in 1933 because at least then the unthinkable was still unthinkable. You knew and you did it anyway. You will never deserve forgiveness for this.

Anyway, fantastic movie. It's easily the first time that Nolan has ever hit this powerful of an emotional cord with me.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It’s kind of weird that you say you remember John McCain and Mitt Romney, two politicians that were never president, and not George W. Bush, who was the one that started much of the norm-breaking that Trump amped up to 11. The rot goes back much deeper than just the last 15 years.
  - You can trace republican rot all the way back to Nixon or Goldwater. That party has been horrid for decades. A real blight on this world. Corporations made money tho 
  - I don’t understand how people are supporting this incorrect take. Bush had his Bushisms but he was a stickler for norms. Yes, he had terrible policy and started terrible wars but he was not a precursor to Trump anymore than Bush Sr. or Jeb were, and both were vehemently opposed to Trump.

Sarah Palin can be considered a precursor, and maybe Newt Gingrich if you want to go further back, but W wasn’t that.
2. trump/ism is easily the clearest and worst “sacking of Troy” we’ve experienced, imho. So much of how the constitution and country worked (as much as it ever did) was grounded in “obvious” human baseline decency and not (yet) codified in law. That has to change now. 
3. I mean, for me I didn’t really connect with that theme from the film because the world has always been cruel and violent all through human history. It’s not a new thing. Humanity has never truly been polite and courteous. What we’ve seen with MAGA is just a mask off event for America but the hate and violence was always there. People in positions of privilege and safety have always been able to live in ignorance or indifference to the cruelty and violence perpetrated onto others. The history of the Americas is built on it, as is practically every other nation on the planet.
4. I think the argument the film is making is interesting but it doesn't really do the work to make it believable in its world. Most of the examples of civilizational decay the film presents don't seem to have anything to do with the trick of the horse. I thought the attempt to make the horse into a violation of hospitality/Zeus' law didn't really make much sense to me because I didn't view the offering of an object as the same thing as claiming guest rights so that connection felt forced to me. And there's an implication that the behavior of the Greeks in the sack of Troy and afterwards made them into the barbaric Sea people that they were so afraid of which again, none of those actions in the movie seemed particularly out of the norm with how I would expect a society in that era to operate so that idea rang hollow. 
  - The way I’ve thought about how Nolan sees the Horse is that gift exchange is a key part of the Xenia rituals in the poem; e.g., if you own nothing, like Odysseus when marooned with the Phaeacians, he has to give as a gift his stories to provide reciprocity for their hospitality. Reciprocal gift exchange is thus the glue that creates trust that allows social cohesion to happen. The suitors also don’t provide reciprocity, but only those on Ithaca know of this. But by creating an (in)famous gift that kills people instead of a gift that binds people together, Odysseus absolutely is culpable for destroying a key part of what makes their world work. It is much like the destruction of political conventions by the you-know-who’s in today’s world.
  - I agree, the whole moral crux of the movie to me felt forced. The horse is a stretch for Zeus's law, and Zeus's law doesn't really resonate for the modern viewer anyway. Plus, are we to understand that the brutality of the sacking of Troy would've been acceptable if not for the horse? Or that it was any more or less brutal than any other warfare of the time? Are we to understand that the purported norm violation is what caused the suitors to exploit the hospitality of Penelope? And how are we to reconcile the gleeful violence against the suitors with the trauma and epiphany Odysseus has had? None of this really made much sense to me.

Plus, the tie in of the bronze age collapse (an awfully prescient thing for Odysseus to reference by name!) doesn't make much sense, since the Trojan War is likely largely ahistorical anyway.
5. Yeah, I liked that Nolans Odyssey is an adaptation, and he changed the themes and characters to follow a more contemporary narrative.

Every age has its own virtues and conflicts. If you create a movie with advanced technology and art, why not also use advanced morals? The world is so diferent from 2800 years ago.

If you want to see the original art go read the book.
6. Not only will the Greeks go to your country and kill all your people, but what’s worse, is that they’ll come back 20 years later and tell a story about how killing your people made their soldiers feel sad.

(apologies to Frankie Boyle)





## 2. How do you keep up-to-date with new foreign and independent films?

**Score:** 31
**Body:** I’m talking about films that won’t have a wide theatrical American screening. Normally I find out about these films through critical word of mouth, but this takes time, and it’s often not for a year or two plus after an interesting film releases, until I hear about it. I’m trying to write more about film, and want to see some of these and try to write before they’ve had as much of a chance to simmer. I know about Film Festivals, but my region is pretty limited in that regard, and I’m really looking to develop awareness of things coming, and not just fully stumble on to things by chance.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The blog WorldofReel posts the slates of the big international festivals, at least Sundance, Berlin, Cannes, Venice, Toronto and NYFF, on the day that those are released. I follow that.

The YouTube channel The Oscar Expert is a pretty good supplement to this first step of getting acquainted with what’s coming up in the big festivals. Look at the channel now, and its latest video is nearly 40 minutes of reading the tea leaves on the announced Toronto International Film Festival’s announced slate of films.

To follow coverage of the films themselves during the festivals, Film Comment’s podcast, and the podcast The Last Thing I Saw can be relatively comprehensive. I think that they used to be even more so, as objectively there are fewer episodes during festivals than in the past, but there’s still plenty enough of the coverage that one would want.

I also watch Peter Bradshaw’s reviews on YouTube.
  - Thank you so much, this is exactly what I’m looking for <3
  - Jordan ruimy (the world of reel guy) is known to be kind of a psycho. Pls don't send traffic his way. 
    - Cannot confirm psycho, just racist.
    - What’d he do?
      - posts a lot of fake info, kinda racist, he's known to harass people walking out of test screenings.

some quick receipts, he's reviled on (what used to be) film twitter

https://xcancel.com/zoerosebryant/status/1635353206741159936

https://xcancel.com/tnyfrontrow/status/1807266100087865648

https://xcancel.com/WillSloanEsq/status/2047893262304735524

https://www.reddit.com/r/oscarrace/comments/y1izji/another_strike_against_ruimy/

https://www.reddit.com/r/oscarrace/comments/w7uegk/can_we_ban_jordan_ruimy_world_of_reel_links_from/
2. look up the classic Cannes/Locarno/venice/Berlin/... schedules + read on filmcomment, icsfilm.org, senses of cinema - but also whenever you watch a recent, non-anglophone arthouse film look for online reviews you like so you can then have new reliable spaces
3. I have a similar issue, though I’m not a writer just a nerdy consumer. Everytime I go dark on social media I miss out on so much. I have a few friends that haven’t used social media in years and somehow they keep up with more than me even when I am using it lol! 
4. If you want an early crack at them and you're region limited, then I don't really see many options. If you want increased awareness of them, just check what's playing at film festivals and also at maybe NYC theaters like IFC Center or Film Forum.
5. I like Reverse Shot myself, it’s the opinion journalism/criticism arm of the Museum Of the Moving Image (although I’ve seen multiple comments shout out Film Comment and that’s a good recommendation as well, you can’t go wrong there). 

If you’re stuck in the middle of nowhere with no art house theater within a several hour drive, you’re probably best off (as others have mentioned) just looking at festival titles or the now playing at the art house in whatever city you arbitrarily choose, writing down what looks interesting, and then renting them on Amazon or whatever a few months after they premiere. Very few people watch those movies in theaters, a lot more people watched ex: The Secret Agent on their home TV than in a film festival or big city art house, so it’s not like you’re missing out on the experience of art. 

## 3. Black Swan and the voyeuristic gaze: does Nina become dissociated from her own body because she has learned to constantly observe herself?

**Score:** 28
**Body:** I wrote an essay for school on Black Swan, so I’ve spent an almost unreasonable amount of time thinking about this film, and I’m curious about an aspect of it that I don’t see discussed as much.  
I think Nina’s breakdown can be understood as a progression from **being watched, to watching herself, to becoming unable to experience herself as anything other than an object of observation**.

The film constantly places Nina within a gaze: Thomas watches her, her mother monitors her, the dancers compare themselves to one another, and the audience watches her perform. But the most important shift is when this external surveillance becomes internalised. Nina no longer needs anyone else to evaluate her because she has learned to do it herself.

This is why I find the mirrors more interesting than simply interpreting them as symbols of Nina’s “split personality.” Early on, Nina uses mirrors to monitor and correct her body. As the film progresses, her reflections begin to move independently, distort, or become separate from her. To me, this visually represents her increasing dissociation from her own body: she has spent so long looking at herself from the outside that her body begins to feel like something she observes rather than something she inhabits.

What I find particularly interesting is that the film makes the audience participate in this process. Aronofsky repeatedly uses mirrors, fragmented close-ups, reflections, and voyeuristic camerawork to make us scrutinise Nina’s body alongside her. We are placed in the same position as the gaze that contributes to her breakdown.

So my question is: **is Black Swan actually making us uncomfortable with the act of watching Nina, or does it ultimately turn her dissociation and suffering into something beautiful for us to consume?**

Because the film seems to criticise the voyeuristic gaze placed upon dancers while simultaneously making Nina’s psychological collapse visually spectacular. Her suffering becomes beautiful, her destruction becomes art, and the audience applauds.

I’m interested in whether anyone else sees the film as being less about a simple “good self vs. dark self” split and more about what happens when someone becomes so accustomed to observing and evaluating themselves that they become psychologically separated from their own body.

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. >So my question is: **is Black Swan actually making us uncomfortable with the act of watching Nina, or does it ultimately turn her dissociation and suffering into something beautiful for us to consume?**

I don't think these are mutually exclusive of each other. The film also is about how the process of perfecting yourself as a ballerina (or anything arduous) is very uncomfortable. And that discomfort is essential for the "consumption" to be powerful and transformative. I don't think it is so much as criticizing the gaze, but rather subjecting us to, and as you rightfully say, involving us in its crucible, that it reveals something very important (and irresolvable) about Art. Art is painful. Art is destructive. Art is transformative. And in some sense falls outside of ethical judgement.

## 4. Casual Discussion Thread (July 23, 2026)

**Score:** 2
**Body:** General Discussion threads threads are meant for more casual chat; a  place to break most of the frontpage rules. Feel free to ask for  recommendations, lists, homework help; plug your site or video essay;  discuss tv here, or any such thing.

**There is no 180-character minimum for top-level comments in this thread.**

**Follow us on:**

* [Letterboxd](http://letterboxd.com/truefilmreddit/)

The sidebar has a wealth of information, including the subreddit  rules, our killer wiki, all of our projects... If you're on a mobile  app, click the "(i)" button on our frontpage.

Sincerely,

David

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 5. Modern film / art and a growing need for 'proof of work'

**Score:** 0
**Body:** The cryptocurrency concept Proof of work basically proves that one person did a certain amount of work / effort to others within a transaction. 

Just made me think of the ease in which people are creating ' ai ' and digital art that is flooding our feeds, our minds, everywhere we look where we don't know who created it, for what purpose and is it based on someone else's work, their own work etc etc

With modern film, the interest from both the viewers and the creators of films to show, hey, we've put in effort in x amount of ways, IMAX cameras, the sets, we were drowning, it was cold etc, the emphasis on showing, exhibiting a certain amount of suffering for the art as it were is becoming more normal and it somehow translates into a new appreciation of the art, even though it was always this way, we're just trying to really show the human side of what it takes to make these images in a world where they are becoming seemingly less valuable

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. >With modern film, the interest from both the viewers and the creators of films to show, hey, we've put in effort in x amount of ways, IMAX cameras, the sets, we were drowning, it was cold etc, the emphasis on showing, exhibiting a certain amount of suffering for the art as it were is becoming more normal and it somehow translates into a new appreciation of the art, even though it was always this way, we're just trying to really show the human side of what it takes to make these images in a world where they are becoming seemingly less valuable

I do think that the spread of AI - and even embodied by the Elum threat to make a Grok "historically accurate" Odyssey - is pushing everyone towards new standards and signatures of authenticity. When currency can be counterfeited easily, the currency loses its value. The Nolan crew now talking about audiences wanting films "made the hard way" is part of that. A big question will be: What new **signatures** of authenticity are going to rise up? It can't just be huge $250 M films with directorial restraint. And, it can't just be running around with your iphone either...because "anyone" can do that, even if Soderbergh did it. Is there going to be layer of films - A24 is trying to market itself this way...but already seems to be losing its grip - that have "proof of work" in their very form? 

---
